mappings.rationaleAI: Western philosophy provides the comprehensive narrative of A as a whole—from pre-Socratic cosmology through ancient, medieval, and modern periods—covering every major sub-path from A.1 through A.3.

History of philosophy spans both the Western tradition (A) and non-Western traditions including Indian, Chinese, and Islamic philosophy (B), making it the most broadly relevant single article for the entire section.

Aristotle's philosophy is the explicit subject of A.1.b.iii, covering his criticisms of Platonic metaphysics, the logical corpus (Organon), his teleological biology and ethics, and his empirical political research.
Socrates and the Socratic method are the direct content of A.1.b.i—the ethical concerns and positions of Socrates and the dialectical method of teaching that shaped all subsequent philosophy.
Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelianism and Christian theology is explicitly named in A.2.c (the age of the Schoolmen: Aquinas's synthesis), making this the canonical article for that path.
Philosophy as a discipline overview covers the major Western (A) and non-Western (B) traditions and their branches, providing foundational context for the entire section.

The Islamic Golden Age is the primary context for A.2.b—the role of Islamic philosophers in transmitting and increasing the influence of Aristotle in the West and the associated Jewish philosophical contributions.

Eastern philosophy directly covers the non-Western philosophical traditions that constitute B—Indian philosophy (B.1), Chinese philosophy (B.2) including Confucianism, Taoism, and Neo-Confucianism, and Japanese philosophy (B.3).
The Age of Enlightenment corresponds to A.3.c (philosophy in the period of the Enlightenment), covering Locke, Hume, and Kant on epistemology, philosophy of science, and social-political philosophy.
Plato's philosophy is the explicit subject of A.1.b.ii—his dialogues on politics, ethics, metaphysics, epistemology, and cosmology, and the role of mathematics in his philosophy.

Cicero's philosophical work translating Hellenistic schools into Latin and adapting Stoicism, Academic skepticism, and Epicureanism for Roman audiences is paradigmatic content of A.1.c (Hellenistic and Roman philosophy).
Kant's critical examination of reason through the three Critiques is explicitly named in A.3.c.i as the defining achievement of Enlightenment epistemological philosophy.

Laozi as author of the Tao Te Ching is the foundational figure of Taoist philosophy, corresponding directly to B.2.a (classical Chinese philosophical schools including Taoism) and C.4 (Taoist philosophy), one of the three priority uncovered paths.
Adi Shankara's systematization of Advaita Vedanta is the primary content of B.1.d (schools of Vedanta: Sankara and Ramanuja) and directly illustrates C.1 (Hindu philosophy).

Ancient Greece provides the historical and cultural context for A.1 as a whole—the city-state institutions and intellectual culture within which pre-Socratic, Socratic, Platonic, Aristotelian, and Hellenistic philosophies developed.

The Bhagavad Gita is a foundational Hindu sacred text; its philosophical content—karma yoga, jnana yoga, bhakti, Vedantic metaphysics—belongs to B.1.a (early Indian philosophical thought in Hindu sacred literature) and C.1 (Hindu philosophy).

Descartes's metaphysics and mind-body dualism are explicitly named in A.3.b.ii as defining content of the Rationalist tradition: the antiempirical character of his metaphysics and the dualism of his doctrine of man and the world.

Marx and Engels's dialectical materialism is explicitly cited in A.3.d.ii as a key development in the empirical-scientific tradition of 19th-century philosophy alongside Comte's positivism and Mill's theory of knowledge.

Galileo's mathematization of nature and influence on philosophy of natural science corresponds to A.3.a.iii (philosophy of nature: the mathematically ordered character ascribed to the natural world, the influence of discoveries in physics and astronomy on philosophy).

The Scientific Revolution is the defining event for A.3.a.iii (philosophy of nature) and A.3.b.i (Bacon's new scientific method, the rise of Empiricism), transforming natural philosophy into the basis for the early modern empiricist tradition.
Machiavelli is explicitly named in A.3.a.i as a key figure in Renaissance political theory concerning the nature and moral status of political power.

Avicenna's Peripatetic philosophy is a central example of A.2.b (the contribution of Arabic philosophy in transmitting Aristotle's influence to the West), and his metaphysics and theology connect to C.6 (Islamic philosophy).
Locke is explicitly named in both A.3.c.i (his inquiry into the origin and nature of reason) and A.3.c.iii (his concern with the freedom and equality of citizens in social-political philosophy).

Dante's Divine Comedy synthesizes Thomistic-Scholastic theology with Aristotelian cosmology and classical poetry, situating him within the broader context of A.2 (philosophy in the Middle Ages, the age of the Schoolmen).

Darwin's theory of evolution contributed to the empirical and scientific tradition in 19th-century philosophy (A.3.d.ii), informing naturalist philosophy of science and challenging prior metaphysical frameworks.

The history of science illuminates the philosophy of nature (A.3.a.iii), the rise of empiricism (A.3.b), and Enlightenment developments in the philosophy of science (A.3.c.ii) across all major civilizations.

The history of mathematics directly addresses Pythagorean speculations about number and reality (A.1.a.iii) and the role of mathematics in Indian philosophical systems (B.1.b), both explicit outline nodes.

The Tang dynasty fostered the Confucian-Buddhist-Daoist synthesis (B.2.b Neo-Taoist and Buddhist thought) and laid the institutional groundwork for the Neo-Confucian revival (B.2.c) that defined later classical Chinese philosophy.

Renaissance philosophy is explicitly the subject of A.3.a, covering humanism, renewed interest in atomistic materialism and Stoicism, political theory, and philosophy of nature as the bridge from medieval to early modern thought.

Romanticism corresponds to A.3.d.iii—the reaction against Rationalism exemplified by Kierkegaard's preoccupation with states of consciousness, Schopenhauer's doctrine of cosmic will, and Nietzsche—as its broader cultural context and intellectual milieu.

Reason's philosophical analysis—covering Aristotelian discursive reason, Kantian critical reason, Enlightenment debates, and the tension between reason and faith—spans A.3.c.i and informs the epistemological threads running through the entire section A.

Voltaire's Enlightenment philosophy—deism, critique of institutional religion, civil liberty arguments—belongs to A.3.c (Enlightenment period) and A.3.c.iii (religious philosophy and concern for freedom and equality of citizens).

Mahayana's philosophical content—bodhisattva ideal, Madhyamaka emptiness doctrine, Yogacara consciousness—belongs to B.1.a (early Indian philosophical thought in Buddhist sacred literature) and C.2 (Buddhist philosophy).
Theravada's Abhidhamma philosophical tradition and Pali canonical philosophy are part of B.1.a (early Indian Buddhist thought) and C.2 (Buddhist philosophy as a philosophically associated tradition).

Liberalism's intellectual lineage from Locke through J.S. Mill represents the substantive content of A.3.c.iii (social and political philosophy: the concern with the freedom and equality of citizens).

Alexander's conquests initiated the Hellenistic period, which is the temporal and geographical framework for A.1.c (Hellenistic and Roman philosophy—developments from the time of Alexander III the Great).

Confucius is the foundational figure of Confucian philosophy, corresponding directly to B.2.a (classical Chinese philosophical schools: Confucianism) and C.3 (Confucian philosophy), one of the three priority paths flagged as having no other coverage.

Rumi's Sufi theology and mystical philosophy represent a distinct strand of Islamic philosophy (C.6), emphasizing fana within a broadly Islamic intellectual framework and connecting to the Mevlevi philosophical tradition.

Good and evil as a moral-philosophical concept spans Socratic ethics (A.1.b.i) and Hindu/Buddhist metaphysical and soteriological frameworks (B.1.a), providing ethical coverage across both Western and non-Western philosophical traditions.

Democracy's philosophical foundations in Enlightenment social contract theory and its connection to Locke and Rousseau's concern for freedom and equality place it within A.3.c.iii (social and political philosophy in the Enlightenment period).

Ethics as a philosophical discipline spans Socratic moral inquiry (A.1.b.i), Enlightenment moral philosophy (A.3.c.iii), and the full Western tradition in A, and its treatment of non-Western moral frameworks connects to B as well.

Ibn Khaldun's sociological and historiographical philosophy, grounded in Islamic intellectual tradition, connects to A.2.b (the contribution of Arabic philosophy to medieval thought), though his primary focus is empirical social theory rather than metaphysics.
